mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive Christianity addresses pre-schism history (A), Orthodoxy (B, G.1), Catholicism (C, G.2), Protestantism (D, G.3), biblical literature (E.1), and doctrines/practices (F).
Jesus addresses primitive church origins (A.1.a) and Christology (F.1.c).
Paul addresses primitive church (A.1.a), post-apostolic developments (A.1.b), and biblical literature (E.1).
Augustine relates to early church (A.2), doctrinal controversies (A.2.b), Christian philosophy (E.7), and the Trinity (F.1.f).
Aquinas relates to medieval theology (C.1.b), theological writings (E.5), Christian philosophy (E.7), and God's nature (F.1.a).
Anselm relates to medieval theology (C.1.b), theological writings (E.5), Christian philosophy (E.7), and God's nature (F.1.a).
Luther addresses the Reformation (D.1) and German Reformation (D.1.b).
The Bible addresses biblical literature (E.1) and exegesis (E.2).
The Old Testament addresses biblical literature (E.1).
The New Testament addresses biblical literature (E.1), Christology (F.1.c), and the Holy Spirit (F.1.e).
Apocryphal gospels relate to post-apostolic developments (A.1.b) and biblical literature (E.1).
Hermeneutics addresses biblical exegesis (E.2).
Theology relates to theological writings (E.5) and doctrines of God (F.1).
God relates to Christian doctrines (F.1), God's nature (F.1.a), and self-revelation (F.1.b).
Christian art addresses art and iconography (F.7).
Christian ethics relates to doctrines of mankind (F.4) and practices (F.6).
Catholicism addresses Catholic history (C) and teachings/organization (G.2.b, G.2.c).
Orthodoxy addresses Eastern Orthodox history (B) and characteristics (G.1.c).
Protestantism addresses Protestant history (D) and teachings (G.3.b).
The Reformation addresses the era (C.2) and Protestant Reformation (D.1).
Catholic Reformation addresses Counter-Reformation (C.2.c).
Calvinism addresses Swiss Reformation (D.1.c) and Reformed churches (G.3.d.ii).
Anglicanism addresses English Reformation (D.1.d) and Anglican Communion (G.3.d.iii).
Puritanism addresses English Reformation (D.1.d) and American Protestantism (D.2.e).
Methodism addresses Free churches (G.3.d.iv).
Pentecostalism addresses Pentecostal churches (G.3.e.ii).
Evangelicalism addresses modern Protestantism (D.3) and evangelical groups (G.3.e.vii).
Quakers address Society of Friends (G.3.e.iv).
Mormonism addresses Mormonism (H.3).
Vatican II addresses 20th-century Catholic developments (C.5) and social responses (G.2.d).
Marian devotion relates to Christology (F.1.c) and iconography (F.7).
Monasticism addresses the saintly life (F.6.c).
Sainthood addresses the saintly life (F.6.c).
Martyrdom relates to early church (A.1) and saintly life (F.6.c).
Angels address intermediary beings (F.2).
The Devil addresses Satan and intermediary beings (F.2).
Miracles relate to myth and legend in Christianity (E.8).
Pilgrimage relates to liturgy and worship (F.6.b).
Science-religion relates to doctrines of the physical world (F.3).
Fundamentalism relates to post-WWI Protestantism (D.4) and fundamentalist groups (G.3.e.vii).
Late Antiquity relates to the early church period (A.2).
Byzantium relates to East-West division (A.3) and Eastern Orthodoxy (B).
Crusades relate to papal development (C.1.a).
Medieval history relates to Catholic medieval era (C.1).
Medieval philosophy relates to Christian philosophy (E.7).
Renaissance art relates to Christian iconography (F.7).
The Enlightenment relates to rationalism's challenge to Protestantism (D.2.c).
The Holy Roman Empire relates to papacy development (C.1.a) and church-state relations (D.1.h).
Stuart Britain relates to English Reformation (D.1.d) and English Protestantism (D.2.d).
Christianity as Abrahamic tradition relates to history (A) and doctrines of God (F.1).
American religion relates to American Protestantism (D.2.e) and modern developments (D.3, D.4).
African American Christianity relates to Protestant faiths in black American society (G.3.e.viii).
Toleration relates to religious liberty development (D.1.h).
Dante relates to theological writings (E.5) and Christian faith in art (F.7.b).
Lewis relates to theological writings (E.5) and Christian faith in art (F.7.b).
Kierkegaard relates to Christian philosophy (E.7).
Secularism relates to rationalism's challenge (D.2.c).
Spirituality relates to Christian mysticism (E.6).
Faith relates to Christian philosophy (E.7) and normative belief (F.5).
Philosophy of religion relates to Christian philosophy (E.7).

The Book of Mormon as sacred text of the Latter-day Saint tradition directly addresses Mormonism, a sect tangentially related to traditional Christianity, covering its relationship to the Bible, New Testament theology, and 19th-century American religious concerns.

Foundational liturgical text shaping Anglican worship order, covering its role in establishing worship practices, the integration of music and ritual, and boundaries between conformity and nonconformity in English-language Christianity.

Covers Christian conversion in Anglo-Saxon Britain through Theodore of Tarsus, directly relevant to the expansion of Christianity in the West and the relation of Christianity to Western culture in the 7th-11th century period.

The English Reformation under Henry VIII and Elizabeth I, the establishment of the Church of England and the break with Rome directly address the English Reformation as a component of Protestantism's development.

Examines the survival of pagan cultures despite the expansion of Christianity, directly relevant to the relation of the Christian religion to the culture of the late Roman Empire and the persistence of pre-Christian traditions.

Traces conscience from Roman identification through early Christian appropriation and Reformation reliance, directly relevant to the challenge of rationalism, Deism, and the Enlightenment and the Protestant response.

Islam's relationship to Christianity including interactions between the Byzantine Empire and Islam directly addresses the relation of Christianity to Byzantine cultures in the 7th-11th century period of growing East-West division.
Scotland's Reformation history and Presbyterianism as a defining feature address the Scottish Reformation and Reformed and Presbyterian churches as major forms of Protestantism.
